title: NxStage System One – Cartridge Not Detected or Installation Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting cartridge detection and installation errors on NxStage System One HD machines, focusing on external causes and safe escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the patient is not connected to the machine if the cartridge is being removed or reseated. Move the patient to another HD machine if treatment must continue.

steps.title: Check Cartridge Orientation
steps.instructions: Verify the cartridge is oriented correctly according to the machine’s guide. Misalignment can prevent proper sensor detection.

steps.title: Inspect Cartridge and Machine Connectors
steps.instructions: Check for debris, blood residue, or fluid on cartridge connectors or sensor contacts. Wipe contacts with a lint-free cloth if needed.

steps.title: Reseat the Cartridge
steps.instructions: Remove and carefully reinstall the cartridge, ensuring it clicks or seats fully into place. Confirm any locking mechanisms engage completely.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions or Foreign Objects
steps.instructions: Examine the cartridge bay for clots, packaging materials, or foreign objects that could interfere with detection.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Machine
steps.instructions: Turn the machine off, wait 30–60 seconds, and power it back on. Observe if the installation error persists after reboot.

steps.title: Verify Software and Sensor Status
steps.instructions: Ensure no pending software update messages are displayed. Confirm that the machine’s sensor indicators are functioning normally (no blinking or unusual behavior).

steps.title: Try a Known-Good Cartridge
steps.instructions: If available, insert a new or verified working cartridge to determine if the issue is cartridge-specific.

CCR.Complaint: Machine displays ‘Cartridge Not Detected’ when a new cartridge is installed.
CCR.Cause: Cartridge seating was incomplete, and contacts had minor residue obstructing detection.
CCR.Resolution: Cartridge removed, contacts cleaned, reseated properly, and machine power-cycled. Error cleared and machine returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Cartridge lot number and expiration date
Whether error occurs with multiple cartridges

title: NxStage System One – Flow Imbalance or Dialysate Preparation Faults
description: Guide for troubleshooting flow imbalance or dialysate preparation faults on NxStage System One HD machines, focusing on external causes and safe escalation.

steps.title: Verify Machine Power and Startup
steps.instructions: Confirm the machine is plugged into a verified, functional outlet. Ensure the machine completes its self-test during startup. Why: Unstable power or incomplete initialization can trigger false flow or dialysate alarms.

steps.title: Inspect Dialysate Cartridge
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat the dialysate cartridge according to manufacturer guidelines. Check for visible damage, dirt, or misalignment. Why: Improper cartridge installation can impede fluid flow and mixing.

steps.title: Examine Tubing and Connections
steps.instructions: Inspect all tubing for kinks, bends, or obstructions. Confirm all connections are tight and correctly positioned. Why: Flow imbalances often result from restricted or misconnected tubing.

steps.title: Check Dialysate Concentrate and Filters
steps.instructions: Verify the correct dialysate solution is used. Inspect inline filters and connectors for blockage or debris. Why: Contaminated or misconnected filters can reduce flow or cause preparation faults.

steps.title: Prime the System and Observe Flow
steps.instructions: Run the machine’s priming sequence. Monitor dialysate flow rates and sensor readings for abnormalities. Why: Priming helps confirm whether flow issues persist after initial setup.

steps.title: Review Alarm History and Machine Logs
steps.instructions: Access the machine’s event log for repeated flow or preparation errors. Document any patterns that may indicate consistent external issues.

CCR.Complaint: “Flow imbalance” or “Dialysate preparation fault” during patient treatment.
CCR.Cause: Observed kinked dialysate tubing and loosely seated cartridge; dialysate filters inspected and clear.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected cartridge seating, straightened tubing, and verified proper priming. Flow readings normalized. Machine returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Tubing checked for kinks or leaks
Flow rate and dialysate sensor readings during priming

title: NxStage System One – Air Detector or Venous Pressure Sensor Alarms
description: Guide for troubleshooting air detector and venous pressure sensor alarms on NxStage System One HD machines, emphasizing patient safety and external checks.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Stop dialysis and move the patient to another machine if needed. Do not troubleshoot the line while blood is circulating in the patient.

steps.title: Inspect Tubing and Connections
steps.instructions: Check arterial and venous lines for kinks, clots, or air bubbles. Ensure all luer locks and connections are fully seated. Proper flow without obstruction prevents false alarms.

steps.title: Check for Air in the Circuit
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood circuit for air bubbles. If air is present, follow the machine’s standard air removal protocol. Air in the line can trigger both air detector and pressure alarms.

steps.title: Inspect the Pressure Transducers
steps.instructions: Ensure transducer connectors are properly seated on the machine. Look for bent, loose, or damaged pins. Confirm the transducer and tubing are dry and free of moisture.

steps.title: Examine the Air Detector Chamber
steps.instructions: Confirm it is properly installed and seated. Ensure no cracks, fluid contamination, or obstructions are present.

steps.title: Swap Components if Needed
steps.instructions: If alarms persist, swap the blood circuit or sensor module with a known good one. This confirms whether the issue is consumable or device-related.

steps.title: Check Machine Settings
steps.instructions: Confirm arterial and venous pressure alarm thresholds are set appropriately for the patient and treatment.

CCR.Complaint: Machine triggered air detector and venous pressure alarms during treatment.
CCR.Cause: Arterial line was kinked; air detector chamber properly seated but clogged with residual fluid.
CCR.Resolution: Removed kink in tubing; replaced air detector chamber; tested machine with a dummy circuit. Alarms cleared and machine returned to service.

helpfulDetails: Verify all connectors and sensors are dry and seated
Note unusual sounds, fluid in the detector chamber, or abnormal pressures
Record final device status after troubleshooting

title: NxStage System One – Pump Module or Flow Controller Failure
description: Guide for troubleshooting pump module or flow controller failures on NxStage System One HD machines, focusing on external causes and safe escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop the dialysis session if in progress. Move the patient to a functioning HD machine. Confirm that all tubing clamps are appropriately open/closed. Why: Avoid patient harm during troubleshooting.

steps.title: Check Power and Connections
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is powered on and connected to a stable outlet. Inspect the power cord for damage. Reboot the machine to clear temporary faults. Why: Some controller errors reset after power cycling.

steps.title: Inspect Pump Tubing and Lines
steps.instructions: Ensure bloodlines and dialysate lines are installed correctly, without kinks or occlusions. Confirm that the cartridge is properly seated and locked. Why: Improper tubing or cartridge installation can mimic pump/controller failures.

steps.title: Verify Pump Module Installation
steps.instructions: Confirm that the pump module is fully seated in the system. Inspect for any visible damage or debris on the pump drive interface. Why: Loose or misaligned pump modules can trigger failure alarms.

steps.title: Check Flow Sensors
steps.instructions: Inspect arterial, venous, and dialysate flow sensors for cleanliness and proper positioning. Look for air bubbles, clots, or obstructions in the flow path. Why: Sensor issues can cause the controller to detect flow faults.

steps.title: Observe Alarm Patterns and Logs
steps.instructions: Note any error codes displayed. Record the frequency and conditions of the alarm. Why: Helps determine if the failure is intermittent or persistent, guiding repair vs. escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Pump module failure alarm displayed during startup.
CCR.Cause: Observed that the pump module was properly seated but the controller repeatedly failed self-test. No external tubing or sensor issues were found.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for vendor repair.

helpfulDetails: Final device status: Out of Service

title: NxStage System One – Heater or Temperature Regulation Errors
description: Guide for troubleshooting heater or temperature regulation errors on an NxStage System One HD machine, emphasizing external causes and patient safety.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Do not operate the machine with a patient connected if heater errors are present. Move the patient to a functioning HD machine. Rationale: Prevents hypothermia or thermal injury.

steps.title: Check Power Supply
steps.instructions: Verify the machine is plugged into a dedicated outlet. Test the outlet with a known-working device or outlet tester. Inspect the power cord for damage or loose connections. Rationale: Fluctuating or insufficient power can cause heater errors.

steps.title: Inspect Heater Cartridge and Connections
steps.instructions: Check that the heater cartridge is properly seated in its slot. Ensure connectors are clean, dry, and free from corrosion. Rationale: Poor electrical contact can prevent proper heating.

steps.title: Verify Dialysate Flow Path
steps.instructions: Ensure dialysate cartridges or bags are correctly installed. Check for kinks, clogs, or air bubbles in the tubing. Rationale: Inadequate fluid flow can trigger temperature regulation errors.

steps.title: Check for Machine Alarms and Logs
steps.instructions: Review the error message details on the machine display. Document any repeated heater or temperature-related alarms. Rationale: Helps identify intermittent faults or patterns for repair.

steps.title: Cycle the Machine
steps.instructions: Power off the machine, wait 30 seconds, then power on. Observe whether the error recurs. Rationale: Resets internal sensors and controllers; may clear transient errors.

CCR.Complaint: Machine shows heater error and will not reach target dialysate temperature.
CCR.Cause: Power outlet verified, heater cartridge seated, dialysate flow unobstructed, error persists. Likely internal heater or sensor fault.
CCR.Resolution: Machine removed from service, labeled Out of Service, sent for NxStage repair evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Dialysate flow verified, no kinks or air
Final device status: Out of Service

title: NxStage System One – Blood Leak Detector Alarms
description: Guide for troubleshooting blood leak detector alarms on NxStage System One HD machines, focusing on external causes and safe escalation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop treatment immediately and move the patient to another machine if needed. Confirm the patient is stable and no blood loss is occurring. Why: Blood leak alarms are critical for patient safety; never troubleshoot on an active patient.

steps.title: Inspect Bloodlines and Connections
steps.instructions: Check all arterial, venous, and dialysate connections for tightness and proper installation. Look for loose fittings, cracks, or clamped lines that may allow leaks. Why: Improperly seated or damaged lines are the most common external cause of blood leak alarms.

steps.title: Examine the Dialyzer
steps.instructions: Inspect the dialyzer for cracks, broken ports, or leaks. Ensure that the blood compartment and dialysate compartment are correctly connected. Why: A compromised dialyzer can allow blood to enter the dialysate path.

steps.title: Check for Contamination on the Sensor
steps.instructions: Inspect the blood leak detector sensor (optical sensor) for dried blood, condensation, or debris. Clean the sensor according to NxStage recommended procedures (external wipe only). Why: Dirt or residue on the sensor can trigger false alarms.

steps.title: Verify Machine Setup and Alarms
steps.instructions: Ensure the alarm threshold is set correctly and the machine has completed its priming cycle. Reset the machine and re-prime if necessary. Why: Improper priming or configuration can trigger a false blood leak alarm.

steps.title: Swap Consumables if Needed
steps.instructions: If possible, replace the dialyzer or blood tubing set with a known good set to see if the alarm clears. Why: Confirms whether the alarm is due to consumable integrity.

CCR.Complaint: Blood leak alarm keeps triggering during treatment.
CCR.Cause: Inspection revealed blood leak detector sensor had dried blood residue; tubing connections were verified intact.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned sensor and replaced tubing set; machine successfully passed self-test. If alarm persists, machine removed from service for repair.

helpfulDetails: Any unusual sounds or condensation in sensor area
